title: Arthur Conmy becomes the fifth senior Google AI researcher to bolt for a rival in seven days
subtitle: The DeepMind safety and Gemini 2.5 contributor heads to Anthropic as the cumulative talent loss pulls roughly $270B off Alphabet's market cap and refocuses the bull case on coding agents.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Arthur Conmy, a senior research engineer at Google DeepMind, announced on X that he is leaving for Anthropic to work on AI safety. Conmy contributed to Gemini 2.5 and to DeepMind's coding work, and his exit makes him the fifth senior Google AI researcher to depart for a frontier rival in the last seven days — joining Noam Shazeer (to OpenAI), John Jumper, Jonas Adler, and Alexander Pritzel (all to Anthropic). The cumulative news pulled roughly $270 billion off Alphabet's market capitalization across the week, even though each individual departure moved the stock only modestly.

What's notable is less the count than the pattern. Each of the five named departures touches either Gemini, AlphaFold, or the original Transformer work — that is, the bench Google built over a decade to win the model race. The market reaction is also unusually crisp about why: analysts are pointing to the lack of a Gemini equivalent to OpenAI's Codex or Anthropic's Claude Code as the wedge that pulls research talent out. Coding agents are the fastest-growing line item in enterprise AI spend right now, and the labs building the best ones are absorbing Google's senior researchers.

The compensation math also matters. Anthropic and OpenAI are both reportedly preparing for IPOs in the next 12 to 18 months. For a senior researcher, swapping equity in a $4-trillion incumbent for pre-IPO equity in a company that just closed a $65 billion Series H at a $965 billion valuation is, in straight financial terms, the trade of a career. The Gemini 3.5 release that Sundar Pichai committed to before end-of-June at I/O has not landed with five days left in the month — and the longer the delivery slips, the harder the talent story is to stabilize.

A takeaway for learners: in technology markets, watch which problems the senior researchers are choosing to work on, not just which company they're at. Right now they're choosing coding agents and safety. If you're early-career and trying to decide what to learn deeply, those two areas are where the most experienced people are voting with their resumes — which is usually a leading indicator of where the next decade of capability and demand will sit.
